original text:
how does it work, what does it entail and why you should purchase this program??
Below is a comprehensive review of autolotto processor.
other software that are based on theories and guesswork, autolotto processor is based on techniques that have been tested and proven to work.
The autolotto processor emulates the personal success that Richard had for over 20 years of playing lotto.
However he did not create this software on his own.
He had a team of competent professionals including three computer scientist, four advanced lotto game experts and 5 staticians who carefully analyzed his lotto personal winning system.
His team of experts designed a computer program that has up to date helped many people win major lotto prizes.
Autolotto processor can be access from any smartphones, computer and tablets.
This software will help you generate lottery numbers that will greatly improve your chances of winning.
It will pick numbers for you that have high probabilities of winning than just choosing random numbers.
The autolotto processor is a highly efficient software that has the ability to analyze winning numbers probabilities on your behalf.
Autolotto processor will pick successful jackpot patterns and historical data and it will use that information to generate the numbers that will increase your probability of winning.
The good news about this software that has been designed by Richard Lustig is that it does not require you buy or download any additional tool because it comes with everything that you need to help you win lotto prizes.
Copyright  © 2008 All rights reserved | .
Simple theme.
Powered by Blogger.

original text:
ANNOUNCEMENT :: Rime Arodaky gowns are making their way from Paris to Minneapolis.
Beauties, this is your opportunity to see our newest designer's full collection!
Rime Arodaky creates a new wave of bridal gowns, breaking away from the traditional wide skirts and corseted bustiers.
With a nod to the 70's and the rock'n roll bride, Rime has unleashed the inner bridal goddess in her designs with sensual details and romantic, yet innovative cuts.
Call 612.361.7175 // e-mail hello@thewhiteroommpls.com for details!
This week will book fast, beauties.
Be sure to secure your appointment today!
Our favorite Americana designer is sending back all of her brand new creations to our showroom and we cannot wait!
Chantel Lauren is for the wild at heart and the romantic spirit.
Her gowns are organic and artistic, featuring hand painted skirts in customizable colors.
From fringe detailing to bell sleeves, these gowns will bring out your inner country rocker!
All of her pieces are 1 0 % O F F all weekend long, beauties.
The White Room is so excited to feature Lola Varma for the first time ever!
This fashion forward Australian brand is for the tailored fashionista who feels most confident in slinky silk or sophisticated separates.
The Lola Varma bride is looking for an | E F F OR T L E S S | elegance on her wedding day.
We are offering 1 0 % O F F these beauties all weekend long!
She stole our hearts with her upbeat attitude and her audacious designs, and now we cannot wait to have her back in our showroom!
Sarah prides herself on a body positive brand and believes that a bride deserves a gown tailored just for her on her big day // that's why each gown is bespoke to its bride's individual measurements.
Also- her gowns are just plain FUN.
All gowns are handmade in Chicago by talented people paid a living wage // can we get a HELL YEAH to that?!
Bridal beauties // Sarah Seven is back at it with her AMAZING new 2019 collection // these gowns are the perfect marriage of traditional bride and modern muse // her daring necklines combined with graceful details are young, fresh, and leave you feeling like a totally sophisticated babe.
The L A W bridal was created to provide a range of gowns that cater to women with a clear and individual sense of style.
The White Room is excited to announce that we will be featuring their N E W collection, full of minimalist silhouettes with exceptional details that are modern, polished, and cool.
F R O M | T H E | D E S I G N E R S // " We encourage our brides to wear The LAW, and then write their own."
// We will be offering | 1 0 % O F F | any THE LAW dress during this wonderful event // AND, the designer herself will be here to help you select the dress of your dreams.
Exciting news, beauties!
New designer to the bridal scene, THENEWHITE, will be debuting her newest collection this weekend!
Come meet the designer, Juliette, sip on some bubbly & see these new gowns in our beautiful showroom!
The fun-loving local ladies of linyage are at it again with a brand new collection!
"... linyage is a design house focused on creating one-of-a-kind, vintage inspired bridal dresses, lingerie, lace toppers and beyond.
linyage dresses are born out of a love for hand-crafted work, an appreciation for charming details, and a fondness for the character inherent to vintage lace.
Like the name suggests, linyage is about drawing a connection between roots and growth...leaving you with a full heart and an heirloom to leave future lineage."
ALON LIVNE has landed in MPLS and we are so excited to announce that we will be showing his N E W E S T bridal collection for a limited time // if you are on the hunt for some serious jawdroppers, LOOK NO FURTHER.
DAN JONES LABEL is an effortless fusion of luxe fabrics, shape defining silhouettes and exquisite craftsmanship.
Call 612.361.7175 // e-mail hello@thewhiteroommpls.com for details!
You do NOT want to miss this weekend.
We are excited to present the N E W 2019 collection of Atelier Pronovias.
Designed in Barcelona, these beauties are wonderfully elegant yet innovative designs.
Made from luxurious fabrics, these wedding gowns are bound to leave your guests (and future hubs) breathless.
The ladies from Brooklyn are back at it with their edgy, yet sophisticated designs.
The White Room will be presenting their 2018 collection and, ladies, these looks are to D I E for.
Come meet the designers, sip on bubbly, and find your dream gown!
Beauties!
If you are looking for that romantic, ethereal & free-spirited gown, Stephanie's designs are the perfect dream dress for you.
Odylyne The Ceremony is a line of exquisite bridal gowns designed by Stephanie White- each dress embodies the deep emotion, sensibility and beauty of the life and art.
Her collections tie in the feelings of the traditional foundations of marriage to the exotic and unfamiliar, while harnessing the power of the imagination to envision and to escape.
The ethereal element is ever present in each style with a twist of romance and deep appreciation of all the delicate moments of the ceremony.
10% off these gorgeous designs all weekend long.
Be sure to make your appointment before the weekend books up!
One-of-a-kind brides // this is THE weekend to find your dream dress.
We are obsessed with Katherine Polk's designs, and are so excited to show our Minnesota brides the chic, emerging bridal trends coming from NYC.
10% off any Houghton gown this weekend- beauties, you do not want to miss this presentation!
Brides-to-be, if you are looking for romantic cuts and beautiful laces in your wedding gown, you absolutely MUST stop by to see the gorgeous Claire Pettibone Romantique collection!
Made with love in LA, these beauties feature delicate lace, elegant silhouettes and the designer's signature stunning backs.
This collection is only around for the weekend, darlings.
Call 612.361.7175 // e-mail hello@thewhiteroommpls.com for details!
Locally-based Caroline Hayden is at it again with a beautiful presentation of her current collection from F/W '16 and S/S '17.
Caroline Hayden has been featured in numbers publications and websites, including Harper's Bazaar.com, Rose and Ivy Journal and Minnesota Bride!
Her cutting edge designs are unique, using carefully chosen fabrics and embellishments.
Walk-ins welcome on Saturday, November 26th // main fitting room reserved by appointment.
Attention, beauties!
This is your chance to see the stunning designs from renowned Australian designer, Mariana Hardwick for a limited time at The White Room!
"... inspired by the grand couturiers of the past, Mariana Hardwick's designs have a distinct contemporary orientation.
Drawing inspiration from from contemporary couture, vintage references, texture and drape, the Mariana Hardwick gown is timeless."
Available for 10 days in the showroom // be sure to book your appointment today!
Born from a desire to create a brand for the modern bride, Marquise Bridal has grown rapidly since their debut release in March 2015.
Forever evolving their vision and the pursuit of the perfect gown, Marquise Bride is constantly redefining bridal trends while continuing on with the fundamental theme of simple uncomplicated silhouettes using luxury textiles.
The result is sultry, romantic and luxury gowns for the modern bride.
This collection will be available exclusively to The White Room for a two-week long exhibition of affordable luxury.
Be sure to make your appointment today to see these beauties!
We are excited to announce a month long showcase with our local gal pal, Chelsea Lovett with Modehaus Designs!
This talented lady has created sleek modern designs that cater to a bride looking for a classic statement.
Call 612.361.7175 // e-mail hello@thewhiterooommpls.com for details!
The oh-so-talented local jewelry maker, Sara Goldfine of Goldfine Jewelry will be showing off some of her stunning pieces during the next month to accessorize your wedding look to perfection.
15% off these stunners all month long!
Gorgeous Spanish designs from Belén Barbero are coming to MN for one week only!
The White Room will be offering 10% off these gorgeous gowns to our brides for the weekend!
Be sure to book your appointment today.
Suzanne Harward Pop-Up Trunk Show!
Nothing looks more striking on a woman than confidence.
Alyssa Kristin collections are thoughtfully designed so that each piece, while varied in style and Silhouette, features a distinctive element - a dramatic train, a unique neckline, a removable skirt.
This allows a bride to accentuate her favorite feature, whether it is her back, collar bone or hour glass figure, and radiate confidence.
The White Room is excited to announce that Alyssa will have her beautiful wedding gowns available at The White Room for an exclusive weekend!
Come look through these gorgeous gowns & meet-and-greet with designer Alyssa!
Call 612.361.7175 for details or use the 'Book and Appointment' button on the right side of the screen!
Appointments are bound to fill up quickly, be sure to book yours today!
Timeless and elegant for the modern day bride, the Rosa Clara bridal gown collection is luxuriously chic and utilizes the world finest couture fabrications.
A bride who wears Rosa Clara will feel unique and special in her beautifully tailored creation.
This is the weekend for brides looking to make a timeless statement.
10% off all Rosa Clara gowns will be honored during this showcase.
Call 612.361.7175 or click "Book and Appointment" on the right side of the screen to schedule you appointment today!
The White Room will showase a curated selection of Elizabeth Fillmore gowns during the weekend, showing off the beautiful vairety of gowns the designer has to offer!
10% off any Elizabeth Fillmore gown during this weekend.
Call 612.361.7175 or e-mail us HELLO@THEWHITEROOMMPLS.COM for details!
We are so excited to announce our FIRST trunk show since opening our doors- Come see the beautiful, newly released 2016 Rosa Clara collection at The White Room!
We will be hosting private appointments on Friday, October 23rd in the evening & all-day Saturday, October 24th.
An open house will be hosted during the day on Friday, October 23rd & all day on Sunday, October 25th for the brides looking to stop in and look through the collection.
Call/E-mail us for more information!

original text:
1883727-34-1 | AMG-176 (Catalog# : 185185)AMG-176 is an inhibitor of induced myeloid leukemia cell differentiation protein MCL-1 (myeloid cell leukemia-1), with potential pro-apoptotic and antineoplastic activities.
1217201-17-6 | A-350619 HCI (Catalog# : 185183)A-350619 hydrochloride is an activator of soluble guanylyl cyclase (sGC).
1253573-53-3 | AMG-511 (Catalog# : 18542)AMG-511 is a potent and selective pan class I PI3K inhibitor.
1186206-79-0 | ALW-II-41-27 (Catalog# : 1842813)ALW-II-41-27 is a Eph receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor.
1321514-06-0 | AZD4635 (Catalog# : 184289)AZD-4635, also known as HTL-1071, is an orally available, small molecule adenosine A2A receptor antagonist.
1229453-99-9 | Acrizanib (Catalog# : 184181)Acrizanib, also known as LHA510, is a potent and selective angiogenesis inhibitor and VEGFR‐2 Inhibitor.
1032570-98-1 | 2-amino-3-methoxy-4-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropoxy)benzonitrile (Catalog# : 184163)2-amino-3-methoxy-4-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropoxy)benzonitrile is a drug intermediate.
1058156-90-3 | Anlotinib (Catalog# : 184132)Anlotinib, also known as AL3818, is a receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) inhibitor with potential antineoplastic and anti-angiogenic activities.
1125758-85-1 | A-804598 (Catalog# : 184125)A-804598 is a P2X7 selective, competitive antagonist.
185991-07-5 | AMD3465 HCl salt (Catalog# : 184310)AMD3465 is a novel CXCR4 receptor antagonist with potential anticancer and anti-HIV activity.
AMD3465 can block infection of T-tropic, CXCR4-using HIV.
1644443-47-9 | Autophinib (Catalog# : 17101617)Autophinib is a novel potent autophagy inhibitor, inhibiting autophagy induced by starvation or Rapamycin by targeting the lipid kinase VPS34.
1124197-79-0 | Adarigiline (Catalog# : 1710167)Adarigiline is a monoamine oxidase B inhibitor drug candidate.
1613589-09-5 | Adafosbuvir (Catalog# : 1710166)Adafosbuvir is an antiviral drug candidate.
1869912-40-2 | AZD-5153 HNT salt (Catalog# : 1710161)AZD-5153 HNT salt, is a 6-hydroxy-2-naphthoic acid salt of AZD-5153.AZD5153 is highly Active against Hematologic Malignancies.
1287665-60-4 | AS-2444697 HCl (Catalog# : 179832)AS-2444697 is a RAK-4 inhibitor.
Six weeks' repeated administration of AS2444697 (0.3-3 mg/kg, twice daily) dose-dependently and significantly reduced urinary protein excretion.
1027847-67-1 | AMG-009 (Catalog# : 17987)AMG 009 is an orally active, small molecule CRTH2 (chemoattractant receptor-homologous molecule expressed on Th2 cells) and DP (D prostanoid) dual antagonist.
1307293-62-4 | ALG1001 (Catalog# : 1783024)ALG1001 is being developed by Allegro Ophthalmics.
ALG1001 has been shown to be effective at regressing and inhibiting new blood vessel formation.
1240308-45-5 | A-971432 (Catalog# : 178816)A‐971432 is highly selective for S1P5 and has excellent plasma and CNS exposure after oral dosing in preclinical species.
1221443-94-2 | A-1165442 (Catalog# : 20178812)A-1165442 is a temperature-neutral transient receptor potential vanilloid-1 (TRPV1) antagonist with analgesic efficacy.

original text:
Man, The Fat Hunter by Miki Ben-Dor | Eat Meat.
Drink Water.
This is an interesting study which argues that our need for dietary fat is what drove our evolutionary development towards becoming the humans we are today.
It is our contention that two distinct elements combined in the Levant to propel the evolutionary process of replacing H. erectus by a new hominin lineage...One was the disappearance of the elephant (Elephas antiquus) - an ideal food-package in terms of fat and protein content throughout the year - which was until then a main calorie contributor to the diet of the H. erectus in the Levant.
The second was the continuous necessity of H. erectus to consume animal fat as part of their diet, especially when taking into account their large brains.
The need to consume animal fat is the result of the physiological ceiling on the consumption of protein and plant foods.
The obligatory nature of animal fat consumption turned the alleged large prey preference of H. erectus into a large prey dependence.
Daily energy expenditure (DEE) of the hominins would have increased when very large animals such as the elephant had diminished and a larger number of smaller, faster animals had to be captured to provide the same amount of calories and required fat.
This fitness pressure would have been considerably more acute during the dry seasons that prevail in the Levant.
Such an eventuality, we suggest, led to the evolution of a better equipped species, in comparison with H. erectus, that also had a lighter body, a greater lower limb to weight ratio, and improved levels of knowledge, skill, and coordination allowing it to better handle the hunting of an increased number of smaller animals and most probably also develop a new supporting social organization.
This entry was posted in Resources and tagged Antropology, Archeology, Carnivore Diet, Carnivory, Diet, Early Man, High Fat Diet, Human Carnivores, Human Evolution, Hunter-Gatherers, LCHF, Low Carb, Man the Fat Hunter, Man the Hunter, Miki Ben-Dor, Paleo, Paleolithic, Paleolithic Diet, Paleolithic Hunters, Paleolithic Man, Zero Carb by esmeelafleur.
Bookmark the permalink.
processed_text:
Man, The Fat Hunter by Miki Ben-Dor  Eat Meat.
Drink Water.
This is an interesting study which argues that our need for dietary fat is what drove our evolutionary development towards becoming the humans we are today.
It is our contention that two distinct elements combined in the Levant to propel the evolutionary process of replacing H. erectus by a new hominin lineage...One was the disappearance of the elephant (Elephas antiquus) - an ideal food-package in terms of fat and protein content throughout the year - which was until then a main calorie contributor to the diet of the H. erectus in the Levant.
The second was the continuous necessity of H. erectus to consume animal fat as part of their diet, especially when taking into account their large brains.
The need to consume animal fat is the result of the physiological ceiling on the consumption of protein and plant foods.
The obligatory nature of animal fat consumption turned the alleged large prey preference of H. erectus into a large prey dependence.
Daily energy expenditure (DEE) of the hominins would have increased when very large animals such as the elephant had diminished and a larger number of smaller, faster animals had to be captured to provide the same amount of calories and required fat.
This fitness pressure would have been considerably more acute during the dry seasons that prevail in the Levant.
Such an eventuality, we suggest, led to the evolution of a better equipped species, in comparison with H. erectus, that also had a lighter body, a greater lower limb to weight ratio, and improved levels of knowledge, skill, and coordination allowing it to better handle the hunting of an increased number of smaller animals and most probably also develop a new supporting social organization.
This entry was posted in Resources and tagged Antropology, Archeology, Carnivore Diet, Carnivory, Diet, Early Man, High Fat Diet, Human Carnivores, Human Evolution, Hunter-Gatherers, LCHF, Low Carb, Man the Fat Hunter, Man the Hunter, Miki Ben-Dor, Paleo, Paleolithic, Paleolithic Diet, Paleolithic Hunters, Paleolithic Man, Zero Carb by esmeelafleur.

original text:
Case packing of cheese blocks | Packing and Sorting Equipment.
Automated cheese packing - The packing process at the Danish dairy, Ullerslev Mejeri, is automated with a new packing machine that handles different types of cheeses and boxes.
The new packing line consists of the packing machine - EGApacker, which handles different types of cheese - round, rectangular and moon-shaped, high or low.
These are packed into boxes, with patterns ranging from 1 to 6 cheese blocks in a box.
For this purpose, Egatec has developed different grippers, which can easily be replaced depending on the type and number of cheese that are to be placed in boxes.
Suction tool is used for the rectangular cheese and different grab tools for the round cheese.
The cheese is transported into EGApacker on a conveyor and pushed onto a product plate where the desired layer is formed.
Meanwhile, the erected box is released from the buffer position.
The gripper grabs the cheese and lowers it into the box.The cardboard magazine, which is integrated inside the packing machine, erects different size of boxes and seals the bottom with tape.
The magazine can be adjusted in width and height, so the operator easily can change between the different sizes.A case sealer - EGAsealer, is also included in the new packing line, where the boxes are top sealed with tape before palletizing.The entire line from box erecting, change of gripper to top sealing takes about 5 minutes to reset.
For Ullerslev Dairy, Egatec has adapted the standard EGApacker, which has been reversed to fit the production layout.
Ullerslev Dairy is a specialist in cheese packing with a wide range of cheese brands such as "Kong Hans", "Stærke Ullerslev" and "Snehvide".
Production started back in 1939 with Danablu and Danbo.
Today, 95% of the Danablu is exported.

original text:
Cities and cyber currency | Crisis?
What Crisis?
← Is the Mansion Tax the best policy on offer for city economies?
A Camden church has started accepting Bitcoin donations.
St Martin's in Gospel Oak says it wants to keep up with the times and has warned that the current financial system is not all that reliable.
I've just checked where else I an use my Bitcoins in Kings Cross.
Clink hostel takes them, but that's it so far.
So how far is the Internet currency from being one more tool in the creation of a localised economy?
Back in the 80s a big new alternative economy idea was LETS - Local Economy Trading Schemes.
The idea was to bypass the mainstream economy by creating local networks of exchange and trading that would be mutually supportive.
These could be local currencies or simple barter systems - antecedents to the currently fashionable idea of the 'sharing economy' - where time became the currency that was the basis of exchange.
Hence time-banking.
In that pre-Internet age the logistical tasks must have been daunting - all that photocopying, keeping paper records, passing information around at meetings or by post.
So I was pleased to find the idea still survives - a LETS network is still in existence and you can find local time-banks from Penwith to Thurso.
A small number of local currencies are still in circulation too, and occasionally generate media interest - Brixton, Bristol, Totnes.
This despite the difficulties in creating notes and having to beat the pavements to generate interest amongst shoppers and shop-owners.
Not surprisingly all are in strong outposts of the transition movement.
Other initiatives have stood the test of time less well.
It's several years since I last used my Big Issue 'Wedge' card to get a discount from local stores.
For the LETS community the idea was to facilitate exchange without a money system at all, and there will always be a place for that idea.
But could the local currencies be overtaken by the rise of Bitcoin, the alternative currency for the cyber age, making feasible an ideal that was practically so difficult in pre-digital times?
It's tempting to feel the social need for an alternative currency is now matched with the technology that can make it a reality: that financial crisis and technological developments have in tandem created the conditions for crypto currencies to emerge.
And that could in turn spur the development of City economies, able to create their own currencies.
It's not obvious why parallel currencies should emerge.
Whether Bitcoin or Brixton pound, they involve consumers in an extra transaction, converting between the 'standard' currency and the alternative.
In the Brixton case its easy to see how the costs of this extra transaction are outweighed by the benefits it brings.
Traders benefit if more transactions take place in the area covered by the currency; consumers get the reassurance that local shops are more likely to survive.
And the costs are very low; there is little risk of not being able to spend your Brixton pounds.
For Bitcoin the potential gains look bigger.
Forex transaction involve the costs of currency conversions anyway, so it's easy to see this being cheaper through a web-based account with no need for a money changer.
Peer to peer payments can also be kept out away from prying eyes of government, to avoid tax or make payments for illegal goods, which has inevitably caught the media eye.
But to be 'real' money crypto currency needs to be more than an internet transactions token.
It would have to move beyond fulfilling just one of the economist's standard definitions of money - a means of exchange - to meeting the other two as well - a unit of account and a store of value.
But this is less technical than it sounds.
Whether a currency is a unit of account or not is really a matter of sentiment: when comparing the monetary values of goods for exchange do I "think" in terms of pounds, dollars, euros or Bitcoin?
This is a bit similar to becoming fluent in a language, when you stop translating in and out of a mother tongue.
Money as store of value is simpler - can I be fairly sure the cash I have in my account today will be worth much the same tomorrow, next week or next year?
How could crpto currencies move beyond being a means of exchange and take on these two extra roles?
Their fate will be the mirror image of trust in State backed fiat money.
Another financial crash, after the stoking of yet another asset bubble, followed by mass loss of savings as governments are unable to afford more high profile bail-out, and we could see trust in national currencies ebbing away very rapidly.
We're now so used to State money it's difficult to imagine it not existing.
But paper money was the invention of goldsmiths in the 16th century, a promise to pay against gold holdings, and as recently as 1921 banks in England were still able to print their own notes - so long as they were located more than 65 miles from London.
The last to do do was a bank in Somerset with a name, "Fox, Fowler & Co", redolent of financial cunning.
From the Bank of England being given a monopoly on note printing, it was then but a short decade to the 1931 break with the gold exchange, marking the arrival of modern 'fiat' currency: money backed by nothing more than trust in the state.
It's enshrining in law - 'legal tender' - gives but a veneer of solidity.
Legal tender has no real practical application.
So a system created less than a century ago has no claim of permanence.
A central bank issuer made sense for a period, roughly equating to the dominance of the nation state.
But it will fail to prevail if a debased, untrusted currency loses its role as a store of value, creating the opening for a modern version of Fox Fowler & Co.
For sure, Bitcoin as set up couldn't the job.
To go viral it would need the backstop of a transparent and trusted institution - something a bit more transparent than the shadow world of mysterious data miners that currently control the supply of Bitcoins.
There's also a debate about whether the 'cap' on Bitcoin creation is dangerously deflationary, based on the economic theory that wages sometimes need to fall, when the skills of those workers get out of date.
Given that money wages are sticky - people don't like taking a pay cut - inflation does that important job instead.
This is much the way public sector workers are being treated at the moment.
This use of inflation to 'grease the wheels' of the economic engine is of dubious morality.
But more importantly for this debate is that it's a technical issue that can be resolved if crypto currrencies bring in some economics knowledge to go with their competence in algorithms.
And perhaps this need for new monetary institutions is where smaller economic units like city economies and devolved nations come in - with local banks creating their own crypto currencies.
How about it Scotland?
This entry was posted in Uncategorized and tagged localisation, money, network.
Bookmark the permalink.
